[AutoTuner Bridge] Passthrough — model merged by AutoTuner (rank #1, score: 0.401):
  optimization_mode: per_prefix
  merge mode: per-prefix (auto)
  merge_quality: standard
  sparsification: della_conflict (density: 0.7, dampening: 0.0)
  auto_strength: enabled

Switch settings_source to 'manual' to tweak from these settings.

nodes.title: Additional notes LoRa AutoTuner
widgets_values:
Tips:

• cache_patches keeps the full sweep result in
  RAM — switching output_mode or re-running with
  same LoRAs is instant.

• diff_cache_mode "auto" speeds up Phase 2 by
  caching LoRA diffs between candidates. Uses RAM
  up to diff_cache_ram_pct, then spills to disk.

• scoring_svd adds effective rank scoring — more
  thorough but much slower on large models. Leave
  disabled unless you need it.

• The tuner_data output can connect to a Merge
  Selector node to try alternative ranked configs,
  or to a LoRA Optimizer for the bridge workflow.

nodes.title: Additional notes combo
widgets_values:
Tips:

• Switching modes is instant when cache is enabled
  — the AutoTuner reuses its cached sweep results.

• In Mode A (from_autotuner), the Optimizer's 
  widgets update to show the AutoTuner's winning
  config. These values persist when you switch 
  to Mode B.

• The LoRA Stack connects to BOTH nodes. The 
  AutoTuner always needs it for analysis. The
  Optimizer uses it when in "manual" mode.

• You can still use the AutoTuner's report output
  to see the full ranking of tested configs.

nodes.title: Lora AutoTuner + LoRa Optimizer
widgets_values:
AutoTuner + Optimizer Bridge

Best of both worlds: let the AutoTuner find optimal
settings, then fine-tune them manually.

How to use:
  1. Run the workflow — AutoTuner finds the best 
     config and merges your LoRAs automatically
  2. The Optimizer shows you what settings won
  3. Want to tweak? Flip the Optimizer to "manual"
     — the AutoTuner automatically steps aside
  4. Adjust any setting (quality, sparsification...)
     and re-run. You're starting from the best
     config, not from scratch.

The two switches stay in sync automatically:
  • "from_autotuner" = AutoTuner merges, Optimizer
    shows the result (hands-off mode)
  • "manual" = You control the merge, AutoTuner
    just passes the model through (hands-on mode)

Switching between modes is instant thanks to caching.

This is the recommended workflow if you want to
understand and control how your LoRAs are merged
while still getting a smart starting point.

nodes.title: Lora AutoTuner
widgets_values:
LoRA AutoTuner

Finds the best settings automatically. Instead of
guessing which merge settings work best for your
specific LoRA combination, it tries many different
configurations and picks the winner.

When to use:
  You want the best possible merge quality and are
  willing to wait for the tuner to test options.
  First run takes a while, but results are cached
  so subsequent runs are instant.

What to connect:
  • model — Your base model
  • lora_stack — Your LoRAs from a LoRA Stack node
  • clip — Your text encoder

What you get:
  • The best merged model it found
  • A ranked report showing all tested configs
  • tuner_data — The winning settings, which you 
    can pass to a Merge Selector to try runner-up
    configs, or to an Optimizer for fine-tuning

Good starting settings:
  • top_n: 3 (tests 3 best candidates)
  • scoring_speed: turbo (fast, good enough)
  • cache_patches: enabled (re-runs are instant)

The report shows you which config won and why —
useful even if you later want to tweak manually.

nodes.title: LoRa Optimizer
widgets_values:
LoRA Optimizer

Combines multiple LoRAs together intelligently.
Instead of just stacking them (which often causes
muddy or oversaturated results), it analyzes where
your LoRAs conflict and picks the best way to blend
them for each part of the model.

When to use:
  You have 2+ style/character LoRAs and want them
  to work well together without fighting each other.

What to connect:
  • model — Your base model (SDXL, Flux, etc.)
  • lora_stack — Your LoRAs from a LoRA Stack node
  • clip — Your text encoder (so LoRAs can also 
    affect how prompts are understood)

What you get:
  • A merged model ready for sampling
  • An analysis report explaining what it did

Good starting settings:
  • output_strength: 1.0 (lower if too strong)
  • auto_strength: enabled (prevents oversaturation)
  • Everything else: defaults work well for most cases

Read the analysis_report output to understand how
your LoRAs interact — it shows which layers had
conflicts and how they were resolved.
dark atmosphere, (cosplay:1.35) , very beautiful and sexy woman wearing (Basic bodysuit and high-rise leggings:1.2) , smiling to the viewer Stirring a pot , sexy pose, in New Yor

nodes.title: Additional notes LoRa Optimizer
widgets_values:
Tips:

• cache_patches keeps the result in RAM — re-runs
  are instant. Disable for large video models.

• compress_patches "non_ties" shrinks memory with
  no quality loss. Use "all" to save more RAM.

• normalize_keys should stay enabled — it makes
  LoRAs from different trainers compatible.

• behavior_profile "v1.2" is the latest. Use
  "classic" only if comparing with older results.

• For edit/distillation/DPO LoRAs, use 
  "weighted_sum_only" mode or apply them via a
  standard Load LoRA node instead.
